read.title: Katabasis
read.author: R.F. Kuang
read.my_review: This book gives Percy Jackson for post-grads as well as a love letter to paradoxes.This book is not at all comparable to Babel, but it is still very much Kuang-esque. I enjoyed the first and second acts, but as with Babel, I didn't love the third act, but still was satisfied with the book overall. I think Kuang does dark academia, with an overemphasis on the academia, very well and that if you treat this book as such then it does very well. The magic system was interesting, but also maybe not utilized as much or explored in depth as much as I would've wanted. Overall I enjoyed the themes of this book, but I have often felt like Kuang writes her books for people like me who share her own interests in a way.I do absolutely think that more authors should be philosophy nerds, and I compiled a list of philosophical concepts mentioned in this book: https://reeswrites.com/posts/philosop...

read.my_review: A book that covers far more than the branding might suggest (or maybe it makes perfect sense): motherhood, feminism, capitalism, and more. I really enjoyed the process of this book, but I did find that she breezed by some of the big ticket outcomes and just accepted their truth rather than really arguing for it. This is more of a personal gripe and definitely shouldn’t prevent you from reading this book since you should make any decisions about her conclusions on your own!

read.title: Attached: The New Science of Adult Attachment and How It Can Help You Find—and Keep—Love
read.author: Amir Levine
read.my_review: This book is a bit old at this point and probably contains outdated information, but I think there are plenty of good concepts for anyone to learn. I think there is some disservice in specifically focusing on romantic attachment relationships, but I think that a good reader can generalize. I personally believe the evolutionary and psychological thinking is a bit misrepresented or oversimplified, but for the most part I think that they did a good job trying to stick to scientific research. For instance, I think that attachment system was evolutionarily selected, but that attachment styles are more akin to something like a coping mechanism and not something that should be attempted to understand through evolutionary psychology as much.https://reeswrites.com/posts/attached/

The only thing that I didn’t really get were the sections relating to parts therapy, but that’s just me.

read.title: Eating Animals
read.author: Jonathan Safran Foer
read.my_review: This book is accessible, inventive, edgy. well-researched and usually well-reasoned, narrative and strange. The most important piece of this book that will be seen as a failing in many’s eyes is that he doesn’t really condemn meat eating. He frames it as a personal choice. This is less about the actual morality of the decision, but that there are consequences to your actions. In a way, this frees the conversation from ethical systems and instead asks a person if they are okay being complicit in a cruel system (factory farming). For ethical vegans this move may be appalling, but I think for people who aren’t as motivated by a personal ethical system that it will resonate with them.

I think she sometimes does not engage with opposing claims too much, but I think that’s alright and just leaves the opening for other work in response to hers.

I think that anyone who is not convinced that social defaults are one-size-fits-all to read this book to get inspired.

read.title: On Bullshit
read.author: Harry G. Frankfurt
read.my_review: I’m not quite sure where to start with this review because it is a bit of a strange case. This is originally an essay (~20 pages) and then was bound in the smallest possible book to squeeze it out to almost 70 pages. I’m not sure if it’s a commitment to the bit or what because this topic of inquiry could be seen as humorous from the outside but is treated with no humor by the author. Instead this is a very straightforward inquiry into the nature of bullshit. I tend to agree mostly with the author’s claims, but I’m not sure why the scope of his inquiry is so narrow. In some ways he seems to refuse to engage with the pragmatics of communication and bullshit, either as a philosophical commitment or because he wanted to be focused in his scope.Either way, this is an interesting essay to read, so if you are interested in bullshit or analytic philosophy I would say to give it a read, but you could just look up the essay online instead of buying the book.

I think that this book overall is great for the normalization of mental health care like medication and therapy. Additionally, it offers us concrete examples of what happens with cognition distortions like black and white thinking and how it actually can affect our moods, relationships, and daily lives.

owned.my_review: This book gives Percy Jackson for post-grads as well as a love letter to paradoxes.This book is not at all comparable to Babel, but it is still very much Kuang-esque. I enjoyed the first and second acts, but as with Babel, I didn't love the third act, but still was satisfied with the book overall. I think Kuang does dark academia, with an overemphasis on the academia, very well and that if you treat this book as such then it does very well. The magic system was interesting, but also maybe not utilized as much or explored in depth as much as I would've wanted. Overall I enjoyed the themes of this book, but I have often felt like Kuang writes her books for people like me who share her own interests in a way.I do absolutely think that more authors should be philosophy nerds, and I compiled a list of philosophical concepts mentioned in this book: https://reeswrites.com/posts/philosop...

owned.title: Eating Animals
owned.author: Jonathan Safran Foer
owned.my_review: This book is accessible, inventive, edgy. well-researched and usually well-reasoned, narrative and strange. The most important piece of this book that will be seen as a failing in many’s eyes is that he doesn’t really condemn meat eating. He frames it as a personal choice. This is less about the actual morality of the decision, but that there are consequences to your actions. In a way, this frees the conversation from ethical systems and instead asks a person if they are okay being complicit in a cruel system (factory farming). For ethical vegans this move may be appalling, but I think for people who aren’t as motivated by a personal ethical system that it will resonate with them.

owned.title: On Bullshit
owned.author: Harry G. Frankfurt
owned.my_review: I’m not quite sure where to start with this review because it is a bit of a strange case. This is originally an essay (~20 pages) and then was bound in the smallest possible book to squeeze it out to almost 70 pages. I’m not sure if it’s a commitment to the bit or what because this topic of inquiry could be seen as humorous from the outside but is treated with no humor by the author. Instead this is a very straightforward inquiry into the nature of bullshit. I tend to agree mostly with the author’s claims, but I’m not sure why the scope of his inquiry is so narrow. In some ways he seems to refuse to engage with the pragmatics of communication and bullshit, either as a philosophical commitment or because he wanted to be focused in his scope.Either way, this is an interesting essay to read, so if you are interested in bullshit or analytic philosophy I would say to give it a read, but you could just look up the essay online instead of buying the book.
